claimed_contribution: The paper identifies two distinct routes to regulatory fit: a process-based route (fit between regulatory orientation and decision strategy) and an outcome-based route (fit between orientation and framed outcomes).

It highlights boundary conditions by proposing a motivation paradox, namely that conditions that elicit fit effects may involve lowered motivation, while the experience of fit can increase motivational intensity and engagement.

It proposes broader consequences of regulatory fit by outlining three mechanisms through which fit may improve physical health and by linking the feeling-right experience to subjective well-being constructs such as flow.
context: The article is set in consumer psychology, especially product evaluation and persuasion under promotion versus prevention focus. It also discusses applications to health communication and subjective well-being experiences.
design_and_data: This is a conceptual commentary/review article and does not report new primary data. The authors synthesize evidence from prior experiments in regulatory focus, consumer choice, persuasion, involvement, and self-regulation (including studies by Avnet and Higgins and related work). The paper uses this literature to develop open questions, boundary conditions, and future research directions rather than estimating a new empirical model.
main_findings: The review concludes that regulatory orientation can be sustained through both matched decision processes and matched outcome frames, each producing a feeling-right experience that intensifies reactions.

Synthesized evidence suggests fit effects in evaluations are often strongest when people are not motivated to process deeply, yet other findings indicate fit can increase endurance, persistence, and self-control, implying different antecedents and consequences of fit.
The authors conclude that regulatory fit may be more pervasive than previously assumed and that clearer tests are needed to separate fit from relevance effects.
one_line_summary: The paper conceptually advances regulatory fit research by organizing how fit is produced, identifying boundary-condition tensions around motivation and involvement, and proposing implications for health and well-being.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior regulatory fit research by integrating scattered findings into process-based and outcome-based fit mechanisms, questioning the fit-versus-relevance distinction, and mapping new downstream domains such as health and flow.
research_gap: The authors argue that regulatory fit research is still nascent and lacks clarity on how regulatory orientation is sustained across contexts, when fit effects occur, and whether fit is distinct from regulatory relevance. They also identify limited theory on broader outcomes such as physical health and subjective well-being.
research_question: The paper asks three core questions: how regulatory orientation is sustained to produce regulatory fit, what boundary conditions determine fit effects, and what broader implications follow from fit. It also asks whether regulatory fit and regulatory relevance are truly distinct processes.
